1.synthesis:
Elihu's third and shortest speech (sixteen verses) focuses on a single philosophical argument: the doctrine of divine aseity (God's self-sufficiency) as the answer to Job's transactional religious complaint. The target is the logical implication Elihu finds in Job's position — “my righteousness is more than God's” (v. 2) — and the transactional calculus: “what do I gain from not sinning?” (v. 3). The spatial argument follows (v. 5): look up at the clouds higher than you. If the spatial distance between earth and sky illustrates transcendence, how much more the distance between human moral action and divine being.

The aseity argument itself is double-edged: sin cannot harm God (v. 6: “if you sin, what do you accomplish against him?”); righteousness cannot benefit God (v. 7: “if you are righteous, what do you give him?”). Human moral action's effects are entirely horizontal — wickedness harms only the sinner and fellow humans; righteousness benefits only “one like yourself” and other people (v. 8). God is not the transaction partner that Job's complaint assumes. This cuts the nerve of both the retribution theology (God rewards the righteous) and Job's counter-complaint (God owes him for his righteousness).

The unanswered prayer section (vv. 9–13) identifies the mechanism of divine non-response: the oppressed cry for help without asking “Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?” (v. 10). Their appeal bypasses the one who could actually answer. Divine patience — God's not punishing in anger immediately (v. 15) — is misread by Job as inattention. The speech concludes with the verdict: Job speaks “empty words without knowledge (hēḇel millîm bĕlōʾ-ḏāʿaṯ)” (v. 16).
1.historical_context: The aseity argument — sin cannot harm God; righteousness cannot benefit him — has parallels in ancient Near Eastern temple theology, where the gods were understood to benefit from human worship (incense, food, praise) and be harmed by neglect. Elihu's position is theologically distinctive: YHWH is not dependent on human worship for sustenance or benefit. The phrase “God my Maker who gives songs in the night” (v. 10) draws on the Psalms tradition of nocturnal praise (Ps 42:8: “at night his song is with me”; Ps 77:6: “I commune with my heart in the night”) and anticipates Acts 16:25 (Paul and Silas singing hymns in prison at midnight).
1.literary_structure: The speech has three movements: the aseity argument (vv. 1–8), the unanswered-prayer explanation (vv. 9–13), and the application to Job (vv. 14–16). The concentric structure makes the aseity argument the philosophical foundation for the unanswered-prayer problem: because God is not a transaction partner, the real question is whether the crier is seeking the right relationship or simply demanding the right outcome. Job's complaint that “the case is before him and you must wait for him” (v. 14) is addressed directly: yes, wait — but with the posture of genuine seeking rather than legal demand.
1.theological_application: The aseity argument is the philosophical foundation for disinterested worship. If God were benefited by human righteousness and harmed by sin, worship would be instrumentally rational — it would pay off. Elihu insists worship is not justified by its returns but by its object. This connects to Jesus's reframing of covenantal motivation: the commands to love God and neighbor are not transactions but the appropriate response of creatures who have received life, breath, and every good gift from the Creator (Jas 1:17). The question “what do I gain from not sinning?” exposes the transactional heart that has replaced the relational one.
1.christology: The “songs in the night” that God gives (v. 10) are the joy available even in suffering — the mystery that Paul experienced when he sang with Silas in the Philippian jail at midnight (Acts 16:25) and described as “rejoicing in hope, patient in tribulation” (Rom 12:12). Christ, who sang a hymn with his disciples before going to Gethsemane (Matt 26:30), is the ultimate exemplar of songs in the darkest night. The divine aseity Elihu articulates — God needs nothing from us — is the ground of Rom 8:31: “if God is for us, who can be against us?” — the one who has everything to give and nothing to gain from our suffering nevertheless gives his own Son.
